summary: Machine policy for whether Windows apps can run in the background. The local Microsoft AppPrivacy.admx file documents the same AppPrivacy policy key and LetAppsRunInBackground enum value used by the app.

setting.name: Background App Execution
setting.casual_explanation: Windows apps can keep running in the background to sync, refresh notifications, or finish work. This policy decides whether users control that, all apps are allowed, or all apps are denied.
setting.why_people_change_it: People block background apps to reduce background activity, save resources, or tighten device privacy.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Force denying background execution reduces background activity and can help battery life, but it can also break notifications, syncing, and app freshness.
setting.professional_notes: AppPrivacy.admx maps LetAppsRunInBackground under HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppPrivacy with enum values 0 for User is in control, 1 for Force Allow, and 2 for Force Deny.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not force a policy and the user remains in control of background app execution.

allowed_values.label: User is in control
allowed_values.meaning: The user decides whether Windows apps can run in the background.

allowed_values.label: Force allow
allowed_values.meaning: Windows apps are allowed to run in the background and the user cannot change it.

allowed_values.label: Force deny
allowed_values.meaning: Windows apps are not allowed to run in the background and the user cannot change it.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes LetAppsRunInBackground = 2, which matches the documented Force Deny state.

windows_defaults.label: Windows device-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 10 and newer systems exposing the AppPrivacy background-app policy

states.rationale: The policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: If the policy is disabled or not configured, the user remains in control.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Systems that rely on app notifications and background syncing
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want users to keep normal control over background app behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to block background app execution by policy.
tradeoffs: Notifications and sync behavior remain available.
Apps may continue doing background work depending on user settings.

recommended_profiles.label: Deny background apps
avoid_for: Apps that rely on background tasks, notifications, or syncing

states.rationale: Use the documented Force Deny state.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want a strict no-background-execution rule for Windows apps.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You expect apps to stay current in the background.
tradeoffs: Notifications, live refresh, and sync behavior can degrade.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft AppPrivacy.admx LetAppsRunInBackground mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file maps LetAppsRunInBackground under the AppPrivacy policy key and defines values 0, 1, and 2 for User is in control, Force Allow, and Force Deny.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft AppPrivacy.adml LetAppsRunInBackground help text
evidence.summary: The local ADML help text explains that background app execution can be user-controlled, force allowed, or force denied, and that not configuring the policy leaves users in control.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes LetAppsRunInBackground = 2 for the current tweak.

decision.why: The local Microsoft AppPrivacy.admx file explicitly defines the LetAppsRunInBackground enum under Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\AppPrivacy, and the paired ADML help text documents the Force Deny state. The app writes that exact enum value of 2.

validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file names the same AppPrivacy machine policy surface used by the app. The paired ADML help text documents the User in control, Force Allow, and Force Deny states for this enum, and the app uses Force Deny (2).
